evidence_quotes.quote: I have seen many videogames that i enjoyed with friends and their communities fall to the hand of business practice that revokes our ability to keep them alive.

An example of what StopKillingGames will help with is Bungie's Destiny 2 game. It being an always online game means that all my 2000 hours spent on it will be eventually for nothing, wasted, as well as all the money i have spent whether for cosmetics, or other things offered in game.
Another third example is "The Crew" from Ubisoft, which is the game that was removed from peoples libraries, directly.
The Stop Killing Games initiative would prevent such events.

paragraphs.text: As a person that spends their time mostly online and enjoying online content, especially videogames, i feel like the Stop Killing Games movement should be taken into account in the DFA. Games are works of art, time is put into them, love and care, and like all other digital medium should be protected as such. I have seen many videogames that i enjoyed with friends and their communities fall to the hand of business practice that revokes our ability to keep them alive. Whether it is the company behind the game shutting down servers, literally deleting the game from my own personal library, or other means of impairing our ability to play. It might sound silly to who looks at it from a perspective of a "non-gamer", but like Movies, like Music, like any medium that is enjoyed by millions, Videogames deserve the same. An example of what StopKillingGames will help with is Bungie's Destiny 2 game. It being an always online game means that all my 2000 hours spent on it will be eventually for nothing, wasted, as well as all the money i have spent whether for cosmetics, or other things offered in game. A lot of people play it, a large community is still hoping for it to be better. Another example is Evolve and Evolve Stage 2. A game focused on coop which was killed off by a company's greed. It was online only with no chance for players to play without official servers. All money that went to it again, wasted and lost to nothing. Another third example is "The Crew" from Ubisoft, which is the game that was removed from peoples libraries, directly. The Stop Killing Games initiative would prevent such events. Prevent citizens from losing money to something they love spending their free time on, and losing a hobby that is shared by billions.
